name: AI Business Foundations
summary: Run your business with AI — plain English, no exam, no jargon. Nine modules from “what actually is AI?” to one real automation shipped in your own business, each ending in a hands-on step on the free Automatos platform.
blueprintNote: First-party skills track (Operator lane) — no external exam, no weights, no readiness gate. Curriculum per PRD-B1-AI-BUSINESS; module prompts in docs/roadmap/ai-business/. Completion = every module's hands-on work plus the capstone (one running automation + a one-page AI operating plan).

verification.notes: First-party track grounded in the Automatos gitbook (the canonical user documentation) at authoring time; every 'Do it now' hands-on step maps to a documented surface, and platform features the docs don't back (opportunity-map wizards, ROI dashboards, hard spend caps, one-page-policy generators, business-ops templates) are taught honestly as learner-authored artifacts or as goals rather than invented click-paths. The platform is mid-evolution — feature flags, Cloud Sync providers, budgets/approvals and role surfaces are all moving — so re-check each 'Do it now' step against the docs on every platform release before relying on it in class.

badge.definition: Completed the Automatos Academy AI Business Foundations track: shipped one real automation with guardrails, wrote a one-page AI policy and operating plan, and can explain what AI can and can't do for a business.

officialResources.title: Automatos docs — Welcome & Quick Start
officialResources.annotation: Start here. Signing in auto-creates your own personal workspace, and on your own workspace you are the Admin — so every hands-on step in this track is something you can do solo.

officialResources.title: Automatos docs — About Automatos AI
officialResources.annotation: The platform in its own words: agents are workers, not chatbots — each has a model, a persona, tools and skills. The plain-English vocabulary the whole track uses lives here.

officialResources.title: Automatos docs — Chat
officialResources.annotation: How you actually talk to your agents. The Universal Router (Auto mode) picks the right agent for each message, so a good result starts with a good, specific request.

officialResources.title: Automatos docs — Agents
officialResources.annotation: Where you build and manage your AI workers. A Playbook is the platform's name for a saved multi-step workflow across one or more agents — the thing you build once your chats are worth repeating.

officialResources.title: Automatos docs — Activity
officialResources.annotation: The command centre: the live feed of what your agents did, plus Missions — an autonomous objective the agents plan for you and only run after you approve the plan.

officialResources.title: Automatos docs — Tools & Integrations
officialResources.annotation: Connect the outside apps your agents can act in. The docs' own advice is to start an agent read-only and widen its tool permissions only as you build trust.

officialResources.title: Automatos docs — Knowledge Bases
officialResources.annotation: How you teach the AI your business: upload documents for RAG so answers are grounded in your own material. Cloud Sync from Dropbox and S3 is live; Google Drive and OneDrive are marked coming soon.

officialResources.title: Automatos docs — Marketplace
officialResources.annotation: Community-shared agents, playbooks and apps — free, workspace-scoped copies you install into your own workspace. There is no paid or revenue-share economy here; publishing is 'submit for review'.

officialResources.title: Automatos docs — Team Management
officialResources.annotation: When you bring people in: three roles — Admin, Member, Viewer. Only Admins connect tools, manage the team, and open Settings, Analytics-Admin and audit logs.

officialResources.title: Automatos docs — Analytics
officialResources.annotation: The cost side of the story: spend per request, per agent and per model, plus projections and top spenders. Note the honest gap — there is no time-saved or ROI number; that half of the ROI sum is yours to estimate.

officialResources.title: Automatos docs — Settings
officialResources.annotation: Admin-only. Bring-your-own-key for the LLM providers (OpenRouter, OpenAI, Anthropic, DeepSeek, Google), model choices, feature flags, credentials and audit logs. Cost alerts on thresholds live here — but there is no hard spend cap that blocks a run.

officialResources.title: Automatos docs — API Reference
officialResources.annotation: For when you outgrow the UI: the REST surface behind agents, playbooks and missions. Operators rarely need it, but it's the proof that everything in the buttons is also scriptable.

officialResources.title: Google — Generative AI Leader exam guide
officialResources.annotation: The business-toned external the modules lean on for AI fundamentals, techniques to improve output, and the honest 'prove it' framing — vendor-neutral and pitched at leaders, not engineers.

officialResources.title: OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (2025)
officialResources.annotation: The plain-language risk checklist behind the delegation and safety modules — prompt injection, oversharing, over-reliance — used to explain why guardrails exist, not as compliance dogma.

officialResources.title: NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0)
officialResources.annotation: A stable, widely-cited frame for thinking about AI risk in an organisation. Used for orientation in the safety module — the vocabulary of managing risk, not a checklist to certify against.

officialResources.title: EU AI Act — implementation timeline
officialResources.annotation: Orientation only: how a major AI regulation phases in over time. Taught as a stable frame in the risk module — no specific compliance dates are asserted as fact, since the timeline keeps moving.

videos.title: Welcome — what you'll ship in this track
